questions.text: What is Turnaround Time?

options.text: The time from when a process arrives until it first starts executing
The total time from when a job is submitted until it completes
The amount of CPU time actually used by the process
The time spent waiting in the ready queue

explanation.correct: Turnaround Time is defined as the total time from when a job is submitted until it completes: Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time.

incorrect.A: This describes Response Time, not Turnaround Time.
incorrect.C: This is CPU time used, not Turnaround Time.
incorrect.D: This is queue waiting time, which is only a portion of Turnaround Time.

questions.text: What is Response Time?

options.text: The total time from submission to completion
The time from when a process arrives until it first starts executing
The time the process spends in the running state
The time from start to completion of one time slice

explanation.correct: Response Time is the total amount of time from when a process arrives in the system until it first begins executing on the CPU: Response Time = First Run Time - Arrival Time.

incorrect.C: This is just the CPU execution time.
incorrect.D: This describes the length of a time slice, not Response Time.

questions.text: Which scheduling algorithm prioritizes jobs based on earliest arrival time?

options.text: Shortest Job First (SJF)
Round Robin
First In First Out (FIFO)
Shortest Time to Completion First (STCF)

explanation.correct: First In First Out (FIFO) schedules jobs based on earliest arrival - whoever arrives first gets to run first.

incorrect.A: SJF prioritizes based on job length, not arrival time.
incorrect.B: Round Robin uses time slices, not arrival order.
incorrect.D: STCF prioritizes based on remaining time, not arrival time.

questions.text: What is the Convoy Effect in FIFO scheduling?

options.text: When short jobs arrive after a long job and must wait for it to complete
When the CPU switches between processes too frequently
When all processes finish at the same time
When the scheduler cannot determine job lengths

explanation.correct: The Convoy Effect occurs in FIFO when a long job arrives first, and all subsequent short jobs must wait for it to complete, resulting in poor average turnaround time.

incorrect.B: This describes the overhead of context switching, not the Convoy Effect.
incorrect.C: Jobs don't finish at the same time in FIFO.
incorrect.D: FIFO doesn't need to know job lengths - it uses arrival order.

questions.text: Which scheduling algorithm is provably optimal for minimizing turnaround time when all jobs arrive at the same time?

options.text: First In First Out (FIFO)
Round Robin
Shortest Job First (SJF)
Multi-level Feedback Queue

explanation.correct: Shortest Job First (SJF) is optimal for minimizing average turnaround time when all jobs arrive at the same time, because it runs shorter jobs first, reducing wait times for subsequent jobs.

incorrect.A: FIFO can suffer from the Convoy Effect, giving it the highest average turnaround time.
incorrect.B: Round Robin optimizes for response time, not turnaround time.
incorrect.D: MLFQ is more complex and not optimal for turnaround time.

questions.text: What is the key difference between SJF and STCF?

options.text: SJF is preemptive, STCF is non-preemptive
STCF is preemptive, SJF is non-preemptive
SJF uses time slices, STCF does not
STCF requires jobs to arrive at the same time, SJF does not

explanation.correct: STCF (Shortest Time to Completion First) is the preemptive version of SJF. When a new job enters, STCF can preempt the current job if the new job has less time remaining.

incorrect.A: This is backwards - SJF is non-preemptive, STCF is preemptive.
incorrect.C: Neither SJF nor STCF uses time slices - that's Round Robin.
incorrect.D: SJF requires jobs to arrive at the same time to be optimal, not STCF.

questions.text: Which scheduling algorithm is best for optimizing Response Time?

options.text: First In First Out
Shortest Job First
Round Robin
Shortest Time to Completion First

explanation.correct: Round Robin is the best choice for optimizing Response Time. By giving each process a small time slice, all processes get to run quickly after arrival.

incorrect.A: FIFO can have terrible response time if a long job arrives first.
incorrect.B: SJF optimizes for turnaround time, not response time.
incorrect.D: STCF optimizes for turnaround time, not response time.

questions.text: What is a time slice in Round Robin scheduling?

options.text: The total time a process takes to complete
The amount of time each process gets to run before being preempted
The time it takes to perform a context switch
The time from job arrival to first execution

explanation.correct: In Round Robin, each process gets a time slice (also called quantum) - a fixed amount of CPU time before being preempted and moved to the back of the queue.

incorrect.A: This is the total completion time, not a time slice.
incorrect.C: Context switch time is overhead, not the time slice given to the process.
incorrect.D: This is Response Time, not a time slice.

questions.text: What trade-off exists when choosing the length of the time slice in Round Robin?

options.text: Too short time slice increases response time
Too short time slice increases context switch overhead
Too long time slice degrades response time
Too long time slice reduces context switch overhead

explanation.correct: If the time slice is too short, too much time is wasted on context switching (overhead). If it's too long, Round Robin degrades to FIFO behavior, giving poor response time.

incorrect.A: Too short time slice actually improves response time but increases overhead.
incorrect.D: Too long time slice does reduce context switch overhead, but this isn't the main trade-off concern.

questions.text: How can you amortize the cost of context switching in Round Robin?

options.text: By decreasing the time slice
By increasing the time slice
By using priority queues
By reducing the number of processes

explanation.correct: You can amortize the cost of context switching by increasing the time slice. This spreads the fixed cost of context switching (flushing caches, TLBs, etc.) over a longer period of useful work.

incorrect.A: Decreasing the time slice would increase the relative overhead of context switching.
incorrect.C: Priority queues don't affect context switch overhead.
incorrect.D: Reducing processes doesn't amortize the cost - it just reduces total switches.

questions.text: What are the assumptions made about workloads in early scheduling discussions (Chapter 7.1)?

options.text: Each job runs for the same amount of time
All jobs arrive at the same time
All jobs only use the CPU, no I/O
The run time of each job is known in advance

explanation.correct: Early scheduling discussions assume: 1) All jobs arrive at the same time, 2) All jobs are CPU-only (no I/O), and 3) The run time of each job is known in advance. These are unrealistic but simplify initial analysis.

incorrect.A: Jobs can have different run times - that's why SJF/STCF exist.

questions.text: In STCF scheduling, what happens when a new job enters the system?

options.text: The new job waits until the current job completes
The scheduler compares remaining times and may preempt the current job
The new job runs immediately, preempting the current job always
The scheduler switches to Round Robin temporarily

explanation.correct: In STCF, when a new job enters, the scheduler determines which of the remaining jobs (including the new one) has the least time left and schedules that one. It may preempt the current job if the new job has less remaining time.

incorrect.A: This would be non-preemptive behavior (like SJF), not STCF.
incorrect.C: Only preempts if the new job has less time remaining, not always.
incorrect.D: STCF doesn't switch to Round Robin.

questions.text: What problem does the Multi-level Feedback Queue (MLFQ) solve that earlier schedulers don't address?

options.text: MLFQ doesn't require knowing job run times in advance
MLFQ is the only scheduler that handles I/O
MLFQ always gives the best turnaround time
MLFQ is the simplest scheduler to implement

explanation.correct: MLFQ solves a key problem: it doesn't require knowing job run times in advance (unlike SJF/STCF which need this information). It learns job behavior over time using priority queues and demotion rules.

incorrect.B: Round Robin and STCF can also handle I/O with appropriate mechanisms.
incorrect.C: MLFQ doesn't guarantee optimal turnaround time.
incorrect.D: MLFQ is actually quite complex with many rules and parameters.

questions.text: Which of the following are rules of MLFQ? (Select all that apply)

options.text: If Priority(A) > Priority(B), choose A
If Priority(A) = Priority(B), run both simultaneously
When a job enters the system, it gets highest priority
Once a job uses its time allotment, its priority is reduced

explanation.correct: MLFQ Rules: 1) If Priority(A) > Priority(B), choose A. 2) If equal priority, run in Round Robin. 3) New jobs get highest priority. 4) After using time allotment at a level, priority is reduced (move down a queue).

incorrect.B: Jobs with equal priority run in Round Robin (time-slicing), not simultaneously.

questions.text: What is starvation in the context of MLFQ?

options.text: When a process uses too much CPU time at once
When interactive jobs consume all CPU time, leaving long jobs never running
When the priority boost fails to work
When context switching overhead becomes too high

explanation.correct: Starvation in MLFQ occurs when there are too many interactive jobs (which maintain high priority due to I/O) that consume all CPU time, causing long-running jobs to never receive CPU time as they keep getting demoted.

incorrect.A: This describes a CPU-bound process, not starvation.
incorrect.C: Priority boost is a solution to starvation, not starvation itself.
incorrect.D: This is context switch overhead, not starvation.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the priority boost in MLFQ?

options.text: To permanently increase the priority of interactive jobs
To periodically move all jobs to the topmost queue, preventing starvation
To reduce the time slice for high-priority jobs
To eliminate the need for multiple queues

explanation.correct: Priority boost (Rule 5 in MLFQ) periodically moves all jobs in the system to the topmost (highest priority) queue. This prevents starvation by ensuring long-running jobs occasionally get a chance to run at high priority.

incorrect.A: Priority boost is temporary and applies to all jobs, not just interactive ones.
incorrect.C: Time slice adjustment is a separate tuning parameter.
incorrect.D: Priority boost doesn't eliminate queues, it just resets job positions.

questions.text: Which scheduling metric is MOST important for interactive jobs (like a web server or GUI application)?

options.text: Turnaround Time
Response Time
Throughput
CPU Utilization

explanation.correct: For interactive jobs, Response Time is most important - users want the system to respond quickly to their input. This is why Round Robin is often used for interactive systems.

incorrect.A: Turnaround Time is more important for batch jobs that care about total completion time.
incorrect.C: Throughput matters for batch systems, not necessarily interactive ones.
incorrect.D: CPU Utilization is a system-level metric, not the primary concern for interactivity.

questions.text: When I/O operations are incorporated into scheduling (Chapter 7.8), what happens to a process that issues an I/O request?

options.text: It continues running on the CPU while I/O completes
It is blocked and moved to a wait queue, allowing another process to run
It immediately gets priority boost in MLFQ
It terminates and must be restarted after I/O completes

explanation.correct: When a process issues an I/O request, it becomes blocked and is moved to a wait queue. This allows another process to use the CPU while the first process waits for I/O to complete. When I/O finishes, the process moves back to ready queue.

incorrect.A: A process can't run on CPU while waiting for I/O - it's blocked.
incorrect.C: In MLFQ, I/O can help maintain high priority, but it's not an immediate priority boost.
incorrect.D: The process doesn't terminate - it just waits.

questions.text: Which of the following are scheduling algorithms that can be used in a Multi-level Feedback Queue? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Round Robin (for equal priority queues)
First In First Out (for highest priority queue)
Shortest Job First (for lowest priority queue)
Any scheduler can be used at any queue level

explanation.correct: MLFQ can use different schedulers at different priority levels. Typically, Round Robin is used for equal-priority queues. The flexibility of MLFQ allows any scheduling algorithm to be used at any queue level.

incorrect.B: FIFO is typically not used in MLFQ - Round Robin is more common for time-slicing.
incorrect.C: SJF might be used, but it's not a standard choice for MLFQ implementations.

questions.text: What is the 'No More Oracle' concept in Chapter 7.9 referring to?

options.text: The realization that we can't know job run times in advance in real systems
The need to eliminate the use of timers in scheduling
The removal of priority queues from scheduling
The end of using Round Robin for interactive jobs

explanation.correct: 'No More Oracle' refers to the fact that in real systems, we don't have an oracle that tells us job run times in advance. This is why algorithms like SJF and STCF (which assume known run times) aren't practical, leading to the need for adaptive algorithms like MLFQ.

incorrect.B: Timers are still needed (especially for Round Robin and MLFQ).
incorrect.C: Priority queues are still used (especially in MLFQ).
incorrect.D: Round Robin is still used for interactive jobs.
